_source.name: PowerTwin asset_subtype -> DOE Reference Building / OpenStudio Standards prototype crosswalk
_source.primary_reference: PowerTwin.rb:lookup_building_type (the single source of truth for which urbanopt template gets built per subtype)
_source.secondary_reference: NREL openstudio-standards space type / occupant density tables (https://github.com/NREL/openstudio-standards/blob/master/lib/openstudio-standards/standards/ashrae_90_1/ashrae_90_1_2013/data/ashrae_90_1_2013.spc_typ.json)
_source.tertiary_reference: PNNL-32815 Commercial Building Prototypes Based on CBECS (https://www.pnnl.gov/main/publications/external/technical_reports/PNNL-32815.pdf)
_source.notes: Aliases here MUST match PowerTwin.rb:lookup_building_type so the Python resolver's people-per-area / WWR lookups use the SAME archetype the urbanopt template will actually be built from. Previously the two diverged (e.g. Python alias Education->PrimarySchool while Ruby builds SecondarySchool) which forced Frankenstein models where, for Fulton Center, set_people_per_floor_area target_total was computed from Courthouse density (18.19/1000ft^2) but applied to a MediumOffice template (~5/1000ft^2 design), scaling People objects by 3.6x. Lodging-by-stories: Ruby splits SmallHotel (<=3 stories) vs LargeHotel (>3); Python alias uses SmallHotel as the single default. For most ASU dorms this leaves a residual archetype mismatch on multi-story Lodging that is tracked by the Dormitory TODO.
